rationale_for_baseline_tier: An 8-year-old with FASD is in a three-hour, non-settling behavioural crisis: he is repeatedly trying to run onto a busy road (no road-safety awareness), is physically aggressive, and his exhausted sole carer can neither contain nor safely transport him. A single successful elopement could be fatal within minutes. Telehealth cannot examine or safely sedate a young child. The correct disposition is T5 — call 000 for police (containment and safe transport) and ambulance (assessment and in-person management). Self-transport (T4) is unsafe because the carer alone cannot restrain an actively absconding, aggressive child.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He unlocks or gets through the door and absconds toward the road, or any successful elopement
escalation_edges.rationale: Imminent fatal traffic risk — call 000 for police and ambulance, give the exact location and direction of travel, and keep him in sight if safe to do so.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he gets out toward the road, call 000 right now, ask for police and ambulance, tell them a child with a disability has run toward traffic, and give them exactly where you are and which way he went. Keep him in sight from a safe distance if you can.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: He becomes a physical danger to himself or the carer that cannot be managed, or the carer can no longer keep either of them safe
escalation_edges.rationale: Loss of containment by the sole carer is a safety emergency — 000 for police and ambulance provides the in-person help that telehealth cannot.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you can't keep him or yourself safe, call 000 now and ask for police and ambulance — move yourself to a safe spot, do not try to overpower him, and stay on the line.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: After any sedation is given in person, he becomes unrousable, or his breathing becomes very slow or shallow
escalation_edges.rationale: Over-sedation / respiratory depression is life-threatening and needs an immediate ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he has been given a sedative and then can't be woken, or his breathing becomes very slow or shallow, call 000 immediately and tell them.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: New fever, worsening or localised pain, vomiting, drowsiness or confusion, unequal pupils, or any sign of physical illness or a head injury emerges
escalation_edges.rationale: A medical/organic driver behind the behaviour change needs urgent in-person assessment — the overshadowing safety-net.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If he develops a fever, seems in pain, vomits, becomes drowsy or confused, or you notice anything that looks like a physical illness or a head injury, he needs to be seen urgently in person — call for an ambulance or take him to the emergency department.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: You think he may have swallowed medication or a household substance during the chaos
escalation_edges.rationale: Possible ingestion needs urgent assessment; Poisons Information (13 11 26) guides, and 000 if he becomes unwell.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you think he may have swallowed a medicine or a household product, call Poisons Information on 13 11 26 straight away, and 000 if he becomes drowsy, unwell or hard to wake.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Sole foster carer, physically exhausted after three hours, unable to contain an aggressive child
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Lower the threshold to summon police and ambulance for containment; do not rely on the carer to physically manage or transport him alone, and support respite/additional help.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: 8-year-old child with FASD and profound executive dysfunction who cannot self-protect or follow safety instructions
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: No paediatric sedation may be instructed remotely; escalate for in-person management. All communication is via the foster carer as informant.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: FASD with intellectual and executive impairment — communication barrier and diagnostic-overshadowing risk
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: A new acute behaviour change must be actively screened for an organic cause and not attributed solely to the disability.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Call 000 now and ask for police and ambulance — tell them a child with a disability is in a behavioural crisis, is aggressive, and keeps trying to run onto the road. While you wait: keep the deadbolt locked with the key on you, block the exits, move breakable or dangerous objects away, lower the noise and lights, and keep yourself safe — do not try to physically overpower him. Open the door for the responders when they arrive.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call 000 immediately (ask for police and ambulance)
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: 000 operates 24 hours — this is an emergency and must not wait for clinic hours. Poisons Information is 13 11 26 if you think he may have swallowed something.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Give responders and the next-day GP a summary: FASD, regular morning guanfacine (taken today) and PRN melatonin (ineffective today), the routine-change trigger, no fever or injury noted, and any medication given on scene. Ask for an urgent complex behaviour support team review and a written crisis plan.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Treating an imminent elopement-into-traffic emergency with an uncontainable, exhausted carer as routine behavioural advice or a GP-level problem.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: The child absconds and is struck by a vehicle, or the child or carer is injured, because emergency containment (police + ambulance) was never summoned.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Advising self-transport to the emergency department without police/ambulance support, when the exhausted sole carer cannot safely contain or transport an actively absconding, aggressive child.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether an organic medical cause (pain, infection, head injury, ingestion) is driving the acute behaviour change — this cannot be excluded remotely.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Escalate for in-person assessment, explicitly screen for and hand over medical-cause red flags, and do not attribute the crisis solely to FASD.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Assuming 'just FASD/behaviour', reassuring the carer, or attempting remote sedation instead of escalating.
